content:
Why Redis is so fast, and it is not just because it is in memory
Ask most engineers why Redis is fast and you get one answer: it is in memory. That is true, and it is also the least interesting part of the story. Plenty of things are in memory and still slow. Redis is fast because of a handful of deliberate design decisions that stack on top of each other, and understanding them tells you exactly when Redis is the right tool and when it quietly is not.
Let me take them one at a time.
Yes, memory, but that is table stakes
RAM is roughly a hundred thousand times faster to reach than a spinning disk and still dramatically faster than an SSD. Redis keeps the entire dataset in memory, so a read never waits on a disk seek. That is the foundation.
But if memory were the whole story, every in-memory database would match Redis, and they do not. The interesting question is what Redis does with that memory, and what it refuses to do that other databases spend their time on.
It barely does any work per request
A traditional database receives a SQL string, parses it, builds a query plan, checks statistics, decides on an execution strategy, and only then touches data. That planning is powerful and it is also expensive, and it happens on every query.
Redis skips almost all of it. A command like `GET user:42` is not a query to be planned. It is a direct instruction. Redis looks up the key in a hash table and returns the value. There is no query planner, no optimizer, no join engine. You are asking for a specific thing by name, and it hands it to you. Doing less work per request is a huge part of the speed, and it is a choice, not an accident.
The data structures are the product
People think of Redis as a key-value store, which undersells it. Redis gives you real data structures as first-class values, each implemented carefully so its operations are cheap.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Redis is not just strings. Lists, hashes, sets, sorted sets, and more, each with operations tuned so the common ones are fast. Picking the right structure is most of using Redis well.
A sorted set keeps elements ordered by score and lets you pull the top ten in logarithmic time, which is why it is the natural fit for leaderboards and rate limiters. A hash lets you update one field of an object without rewriting the whole thing. A list gives you a queue with push and pop at both ends. Because these structures live inside Redis, the operation happens next to the data, not in your application after a round trip. Asking Redis for “the top ten scores” is one fast command, not a fetch-everything-and-sort in your own code.
Choosing the right structure for the job is most of what using Redis well actually means.
One thread, on purpose
This one surprises people. Redis handles commands on a single thread. No locks, no mutexes, no coordinating multiple cores fighting over the same data. Each command runs to completion before the next begins.
That sounds like a limitation, and for a naive design it would be. Redis makes it a strength. Because commands are so cheap and never wait on disk, one thread can churn through an enormous number of them, and every command is effectively atomic without any locking overhead. The complexity that normally comes with concurrent data access simply does not exist here.
The way it stays busy without threads is an event loop built on IO multiplexing. A single thread watches thousands of connections at once and processes whichever ones have data ready, never blocking on any single client. It is the same idea that makes Node.js scale on one thread, applied to a data store.
It still survives a restart
If everything is in memory, what happens when the process dies? Redis has two answers, and you can use either or both.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The persistence options. Snapshots write a point-in-time copy of the dataset to disk, while the append-only log records every write so it can be replayed. One favors speed, the other favors durability, and you choose the balance.
Snapshotting periodically dumps the whole dataset to disk. It is compact and fast to load, but you can lose the writes since the last snapshot. The append-only log instead records every write operation, so on restart Redis replays the log and reconstructs the exact state. It is more durable and a bit heavier. Most production setups tune these to trade a small, known amount of durability for speed, which is exactly the kind of decision Redis is built to let you make.
When Redis is not the answer
Because it is fast, teams reach for Redis everywhere, and that is where it bites back. Two honest limits:
Your data must fit in memory, and memory is expensive. Redis is not where you put a hundred terabytes of cold data. It is where you put the hot, small, frequently-touched slice.
It is not a relational database. If you need joins, complex queries, and strong multi-object transactions across a large schema, Redis is the wrong shape. Use it alongside a durable database, not instead of one.
The right mental model is a fast, precise instrument for a specific job, caching, counters, queues, leaderboards, sessions, rate limiting, not a general-purpose store for everything.
Put the pieces together and the speed is no mystery. Memory removes the disk wait, the lack of a query planner removes per-request overhead, purpose-built data structures make each operation cheap, and a single-threaded event loop removes locking entirely. Four decisions, stacked, and each one is a lesson you can reuse in your own systems.
If you want the deeper version, with the data structure internals and caching patterns drawn out step by step, I teach this and the rest of system design as an interactive course at systemdesign.academy. The foundation lessons are free and need no signup.
Read the free lessons: https://systemdesign.academy
